publications.editedComment: Oh—those fixtures are not lost, exactly. They are the records beginning with `TestComponent-` or `TestComponent_` whose folder or title also marks them as tests. The first button performs only a dry run and lists each candidate id, folder, creation time, and attached Reaction count. Deletion stays disabled until candidates exist and the curator checks the explicit confirmation that both Components and their Reactions may be removed. Apply sends only the reviewed candidate ids back to the cleanup endpoint; success refreshes the Component store. Errors remain visible, and a dry run finding nothing says so plainly. I did not want to presume, but the safeguards were under the warning panel the whole time: narrow matching, inspect first, confirm, then apply.

The deep does not discard a ruin merely because it resembles another. This panel searches narrowly for historical Cypress records: the Component name must begin with one of two declared test prefixes, and the folder or title must also identify a fixture. Dry run comes first. Each surfaced record carries its id, folder, creation time, and the number of Reactions that would descend with it. Only after the curator reviews those ids and marks the confirmation may Apply send the exact candidate list to the cleanup endpoint. A successful purge refreshes the Component store; failure leaves the evidence visible. Nothing outside the returned set is touched. The sea takes patiently and completely. This tool, more mercifully, asks twice.

Every color has a price in attention, even when the palette is free. On mount, the studio loads the mural definition, runs the one-time legacy-state translation, opens that page in the Coloring store, and hands the definition to the canvas. Saved colors may be selected; custom ones may be added by name and value or removed, while definition palette colors remain. Clicking a section both paints and selects it. Groups are assembled from the definition in source order and marked mixed when their section fills differ; a whole group can receive the active color before one section is overridden. Undo and Reset delegate to the store. Save image asks the canvas for a Blob and downloads it, guarding against duplicate exports. That ledger would price it as one page, many fills, and no hidden brushwork.

The mural waits, as proper surfaces do, until its definition is loaded. Then the ancient state is translated once, the Coloring store opens the page, and the canvas receives the current fills, selected regions, stroke width, and palette resolver. Mortals may choose saved colors, add their own, remove only those custom additions, paint one section, or fill an entire group before refining individual shapes. The group list preserves the definition's order and distinguishes one-color groups from mixed ones. Undo and Reset return to the store; Save image exports the canvas Blob while preventing a second export from interrupting the first. Sensible. Even gods prefer a palette whose base colors cannot be casually thrown away. The listener is still being assessed, but the brush controls may remain.

Here's the angle, and yeah, the ears are twitchin', I know. The Component renders only a hidden span because the work is synchronization. It reads `route.query.component`, normalizes it, and resolves that value against whichever Component-store list is populated. No query clears the selected Component. An unresolved query is left alone until records exist; then selection is cleared and the bad parameter is removed with `router.replace`. A valid match updates the store and replaces noncanonical query text with the stable canonical value. The second watcher runs the other direction: store selection changes push a matching query while preserving path, other query fields, and hash. Equal ids stop the loop. One route, one selected record, two watchers checking each other's story. That's as close to a clean exit as routing gets.

The router is quiet because it renders only a hidden marker. Its hospitality is in keeping the URL and Component store from speaking past one another. One watcher normalizes the route's `component` query, resolves it against the available Component records, clears invalid selections once the records are loaded, and replaces noncanonical values without adding history. The other watches the selected Component id and pushes the corresponding canonical query when the store changes. Both preserve the current path, unrelated query values, and hash; matching ids require no movement. An absent query means no selected exhibit. No data is fetched or persisted here. Steam hides many things, but not the guest's room number: the route may name the selection, and the selection may update the route, each without serving itself twice.
